- [S3.Client.download_file()](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.download_file)
- [S3.Client.download_fileobj()](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.download_fileobj)

https://github.com/apache/airflow/blob/4a5e247536b2b7cb304f2f8dc5961d94278affe1/airflow/providers/amazon/aws/hooks/s3.py#L869-L877

At that moment in this method first we try to check if object exists before download and raise an error if not exists.

Might be better do not use `boto3.resource("s3").Object` at all and just try to download object by `boto3.client("s3")` instead if any error happen we still can catch them and inform users.

```python
        with NamedTemporaryFile(dir=local_path, prefix='airflow_tmp_', delete=False) as local_tmp_file:
```

A few things are getting conflated in this thread.
#### 1) The PR code change:
It looks like you're just unpacking the contents of `extra_args` into the `load` call. I agree with @Taragolis that I'm not sure `obj.load` expects all the things it could receive from that dict (or if any of them would even help with encryption). Object.load ([docs](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Object.load)) basically just calls s3client `head_object` ([docs](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.head_object)) which is one of those weird S3 APIs:
>A HEAD request has the same options as a GET action on an object. The response is identical to the GET response except that there is no response body. Because of this, if the HEAD request generates an error, it returns a generic 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden code. It is not possible to retrieve the exact exception beyond these error codes.

To handle encryption when using that API you have to set request headers (see again the previous doc link):

>If you encrypt an object by using server-side encryption with customer-provided encryption keys (SSE-C) when you store the object in Amazon S3, then when you retrieve the metadata from the object, you must use the following headers:

    x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-algorithm
    x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key
    x-amz-server-side-encryption-customer-key-MD5

#### 2) Andrey's suggested change:

> At that moment in this method first we try to check if object exists before download and raise an error if not exists.
This check could cause an issues in some cases.

Just so that I fully understand your proposal, what are some cases you're thinking of? Also this change seems out of scope for what @moreaupascal56 is trying to achieve?

Also, also, for your suggested code snippet, you're explicitly closing and unlinking the temp file, do you not just the context manager to do so?
> Just so that I fully understand your proposal, what are some cases you're thinking of?

Both general case and @moreaupascal56 case. My idea is just use more common pythonic approach: **Easier to Ask for Forgiveness than Permission**. 

Most of the code was add about 2 years ago and most probably it just migrated from < Airflow 2 and I thought current implementation do not cover all use cases.

Initially by selected method tried to get information about object but do not pass any of the `boto3.s3.transfer` Download Arguments there:  `'ChecksumMode', 'VersionId', 'SSECustomerAlgorithm', 'SSECustomerKey', 'SSECustomerKeyMD5', 'RequestPayer', 'ExpectedBucketOwner'`.

If user want specific _VersionId_ it actually check latest _VersionId_ or delete marker (need to check just my thoughts).

Example
```
hook = S3Hook(aws_conn_id="aws_default", extra_args={"VersionId": "AAAAAAAA"})
hook.download_file(key="some/awesome/key.parquet", bucket_name="awesome-bucket")
```

So what might happen here?
`s3_obj = self.get_key(key, bucket_name)` might return object with Version ID = "AAAAAAAA" or "BBBBBBBB"

After that we tried to download file and pass ExtraArgs = `{"VersionId": "AAAAAAAA"}` and I thought it is possible get some funny error if `s3_obj` referenced to Version ID = "BBBBBBBB".

So my idea get rid of call `self.get_key` and just use [S3.Client.download_fileobj()](https://boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/reference/services/s3.html#S3.Client.download_fileobj) instead which might handle passing correct headers for customer-provided encryption and/or Version ID

> you're explicitly closing and unlinking the temp file, do you not just the context manager to do so?

As I understand current code correct `NamedTemporaryFile` use for create unique file inside Airflow Worker, and explicit set that this file shouldn't be deleted because this method returns file path to downloaded file.

In my snippet I just add manually close file if any error happen because we still in context manager and unlink them because if some error happen this file not useful anymore and we could remove it.
Some sample of error which could keep some unwanted files, all of them not related to permission:
- Connection error in the middle of download file
- User tried download 20 GiB file inside Fargate temp directory and get "No space left on device"

If everything alright than context manager close the file
The context manager will clean up the file even in the case of exception (see [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/28158006/1055702)) so there is no need to do any file handling in a finally. This is the beauty of Context Managers! :smiley:

I'm not 100% confident with k8s but I believe the answer to your question is yes, they are serving the same functional purpose here?

A task ARN is essentially the UUID (or maybe a URI is more accurate) assigned to the task by the ECS cluster when it launches the task.  In reality, the value here is going to be in the format `arn:aws:{service_name}:{region}:{account_number}:task/{cluster_name}/{task_uuid}`, so something like `arn:aws:ecs:us-east-1:8675309:task/ecs-executor-cluster/4d080c8e273f4d47af3037793084f6a8`.  For the purpose of the test, the exact format is irrelevant so I used 001 to make it easier to read.

In the task adoption code, we are using that ARN as the external_executor_id because it is a globally unique value and  that is how we can re-connect to that particular task to monitor it.

_One worthy note for reviewer (as the diff is a bit hard to read unfortunately)_: Previously, this function _always_ asserted that there is the `super !== null` check before `super` is invoked. This assertion won't be generated in the downleveled ES2015 output as the constructor seems explicitly called to TS when it processes the ES2015 output to ES5.

I've updated the logic to skip the `super !== null` check if there is no binary expression, but if there is, then we still enforce that this is actually the expected `super !== null` check. I'm not sure of any risk here as we even ensure that there are no parameters on the containing constructor (for which we perform this check).

In this playbook, only the non-failed host executes the task after clear_host_errors:
```yaml
---
- hosts: localhost
  gather_facts: no
  tasks:
    - add_host: name={{ item }} ansible_connection=local
      loop: [h1, h2]

- hosts: h1,h2
  gather_facts: no
  handlers:
    - name: sometimes_fail
      fail:
      when: 'inventory_hostname == "h1"'
  tasks:
    - command: echo
      notify: sometimes_fail
    - meta: flush_handlers
    - meta: clear_host_errors
    - debug: msg='should run for both hosts'
```

I think your fix is the correct thing to do.

> After thinking about it more, making a handler failure recoverable via clear_host_errors doesn't make sense since it's the last iterator state. Having a flush_handlers midplay is confusing me.

The confusing part is that technically `IteratingStates.HANDLERS` is actually not the last iterator state. If you look at the state machine in the `PlayIterator` there is nothing that would advance the state into `IteratingStates.HANDLERS`, no `state.run_state = IteratingStates.HANDLERS`. Only thing that makes it "last" is that we have implicit `flush_handlers` tasks after each section. And `flush_handlers` is the only way for `PlayIterator` to get into `IteratingStates.HANDLERS` state. And what makes this hard (and confusing) is that you can run `flush_handlers` anywhere in the play 🤯 
Although I wonder if we should think of this as clear_host_errors issue and deal with it in a separate PR?

the behavior on devel matches this branch (the final `- debug: msg='should run for both hosts'` still only runs for h2), and that definitely seems like a bug to me.

This pattern is `O(NM)` with the number of properties on the eventargs type * number of incoming values. I know that `N` is fixed, but this is still a lot of `NameEquals` calls with `N=M=14` here. At what point do you think it would be worth amortizing that by creating a `SortedDictionary<JsonEncodedText, Action<MouseEventArgs, JsonValue>>` or similar up front? It's still `O(log(N) * M)` where `N` is fixed in the framework, so maybe it's not a big enough difference to complicate our lives with.

Separately, a hostile user could send an event payload like `{"a":0,"b":0,"c":0,..."zzz":0}` and force a lot of string comparisons per event. I estimate about 57,000 string comparisons per event based on `N=14` and `M=~4000` if we allow a 32KB message and they use two-char property name combinations using chars in `a-zA-Z0-9`. Is it worth counting the number of properties we've considered, and throwing if it gets above (say) 50? That wouldn't make the code much more complex.
In fact, would it be valid to throw if we see *any* property name that we didn't expect? Our JS client sends a known set, doesn't it (it doesn't just serialize raw JS `Event` objects, does it)?
> In fact, would it be valid to throw if we see any property name that we didn't expect?

We could. It'd be different to the current behavior (values without corresponding C# properties get ignored by the serializer) but that might be fine since it would indicate a bug or suggest that we could be sending less data from JS if we genuinely don't need it. 
Adding a throw for unknown properties to all of the readers doesn't cause any of our tests to fail, so perhaps that's a sufficient solution to limit this sort of attack vector. 
I'm not sure there's a security concern here, there's a limit to the number of operations you can force and its not controlled by the end user. If we want to make the implementation more "scalable" that's fine, but I don't think we should do anything in the name of security.

There's only an issue when you can cause the work performed by the server to grow without growing your own work. (For example, if you have an input where you enter a number and the server calculates the factorial or fibonacci of that number as an example), but in general it's ok if you have to do more work to cause the server to do more work, even if the scale is different. At that point, its up to the server to establish the appropriate limits of what it considers acceptable.

In this case, even if this can be 57K string comparisons in the worst case, I think it'll be fast enough that it won't matter. It might even be the case, that this way is even faster than doing a hash and a lookup on a Dictionary.
I was suggesting a structure that would seek via a series of comparisons, not a hash, but in any case it sounds like we’re all settled on the pattern here with the sequence of Equals checks.

just curious, are these defaults we are making up? or there's some RFC or something we should ref?
I didn't see any suggested default for the refresh token lifetime in the oauth2 RFC [1] or any suggestion that it necessarily needs to expire at all.

Microsoft identity platform default to 24 hours for SPAs and 90 days for everything else [2]. Auth0 defaults to 30 days [3]. And our Cookie auth handler's cookie expiration defaults to 14 days [4].

Since cookies have sliding expiration similar to refresh by default, and cookies and refresh tokens are created the same way by default, I went with the Cookie auth handler's cookie expiration of 14 days.

4. https://github.com/dotnet/aspnetcore/blob/e4c6621d9a09cae8565ad918ba5083c9b28840bd/src/Security/Authentication/Cookies/src/CookieAuthenticationOptions.cs#L30

As it turns out, there were performance regressions for JAX. They are now fixed (and benchmarked).

The reason why there was no regression for Torch even though the prefetching code was removed is that the implementation for the prefetching used a synchronous function to transfer from CPU to GPU. In comparison, the JAX implementation uses an asynchronous function. I'm playing with some asynchronous way of transferring tensors from CPU to GPU for Torch, but it's not quite working right now (it's much slower for some reason), so I'll address that in a separate PR.

Here are the results (very reproducible, numbers are within ± 1ms).

Notes:
- I tweaked the model size so that the preprocessing on CPU for a step takes about the same time as one step of training on GPU to make the lack of prefetching most detectable (see code below).
- Only the TFDataset benchmark uses preprocessing.

### JAX

| DataAdapter type|Arrays|Generator|PyDataset|TFDataset|DataLoader|
|------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| before this PR      | 222 ms | 222 ms | 222 ms | 259 ms | 244 ms |
| after this PR         | 222 ms | 222 ms | 222 ms | 259 ms | 244 ms |

(no changes)

### Torch

| DataAdapter type|Arrays|Generator|PyDataset|TFDataset|DataLoader|
|------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| before this PR      | 366 ms | 366 ms | 366 ms | 384 ms | 359 ms |
| after this PR         | 353 ms | 366 ms | 366 ms | 384 ms | 359 ms |

`ArrayDataAdapter` is bit faster now because it has a native Torch implementation.

### Test code

Based on @james77777778 's test code in https://github.com/keras-team/keras/pull/18991 but I added coverage for all `DataAdapter` types.

```python
import numpy as np

# isort: off
import torch  # noqa:F401 dummy import to prevent segmentation fault
import tensorflow as tf
# isort: on

import keras
from keras import backend
from keras import layers
from keras import losses
from keras import optimizers
from keras.trainers.data_adapters.py_dataset_adapter import PyDataset

class TestModel(keras.Model):
    def __init__(self, num_layers=4, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.num_layers = num_layers
        self.convs = []
        for _ in range(num_layers):
            self.convs.append(
                layers.Conv2D(32, 3, padding="same", activation="relu")
            )
        self.pool = layers.GlobalAveragePooling2D()
        self.fatten = layers.Flatten()
        self.dense = layers.Dense(1)

    def call(self, x):
        for i in range(self.num_layers):
            x = self.convs[i](x)
        x = self.pool(x)
        x = self.fatten(x)
        x = self.dense(x)
        return x

def preprocess(x, y):
    x = tf.image.random_brightness(x, 0.2)
    x = tf.image.random_contrast(x, 0.2, 0.5)
    x = tf.image.random_saturation(x, 0.2, 0.5)
    x = tf.image.rgb_to_hsv(x)
    x = tf.image.hsv_to_rgb(x)
    return x, y

model = TestModel(num_layers=8)
model.compile(
    optimizer=optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.001),
    loss=losses.MeanSquaredError(),
    jit_compile=False if backend.backend() == "torch" else True,
)

x = np.random.random((1024, 416, 416, 3)).astype("float32")
y = np.random.random((1024, 1)).astype("float32")

print("=== fit with Arrays ===")
model.fit(x, y, batch_size=32, epochs=3)

print("=== fit with Generator ===")
def generate_data():
    while True:
        for idx in range(32):
            low = idx * 32
            high = low + 32
            yield x[low:high], y[low:high]

model.fit(generate_data(), steps_per_epoch=32, epochs=3)

print("=== fit with PyDataset ===")
class ExamplePyDataset(PyDataset):
    def __len__(self):
        return 32

    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        low = idx * 32
        high = low + 32
        return x[low:high], y[low:high]

model.fit(ExamplePyDataset(), epochs=3)

print("=== fit with TFDataset ===")
tf_dataset = tf.data.Dataset.from_tensor_slices((x, y))
tf_dataset = tf_dataset.batch(32)
tf_dataset = tf_dataset.map(preprocess, num_parallel_calls=tf.data.AUTOTUNE)
tf_dataset = tf_dataset.prefetch(tf.data.AUTOTUNE)
model.fit(tf_dataset, epochs=3)

print("=== fit with Torch DataLoader ===")
torch_dataset = torch.utils.data.TensorDataset(torch.as_tensor(x), torch.as_tensor(y))
torch_dataloader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(torch_dataset, batch_size=32)
model.fit(torch_dataloader, epochs=3)
```

I redo my analysis and I understand my mistake while trying to fix the issue raised during the test on Github.

If I call `data.release()` (on FileUpload item) before `cleanAllHttpData()`:
- In Memory: OK final refCnt is 0 on `undecodedChunk` 
- On Disk : KO since when calling cleanAllHttpData, the HttpData item refCnt is already 0 and the exception is raised due to IllegalReferenceCountException

If I only call `cleanAllHttpData()`: 
- In Memory: the FileUpload item is not released (`undecodedChunk` keeps all refCnt)
   -  This is where the issue was raised during tests.
- On Disk: OK final refCnt is 0 on `undecodedChunk` 

According to method definition of `cleanAllHttpData()`, this is normal since it only concerns disk based attributes.

To resume, the issue is when using Disk based Factory, calling for each HttpData `release()` method before `decoder.destroy()` raises an exception during the destroy since refCnt is already 0. Calling `cleanAllHttpData()` is not an issue.
Doing `release()` for each HttpData before `decoder.destroy()` raises no exception when using Memory based factory, but not calling `release()` will not release all buffers when decoder is destroy, so refCnt is still > 0.

So you are perfectly right, this is not a good solution.
So I revert my temptative to tend to be homogeneous on both but having a Mixed mode factory could be not obvious (when to call realease or not?), so my temptative to prevent to try to decrease refCnt when already 0.
I think we should try to release if possible during destroy (Disk based is done this way too, but not Memory based) to be consistent.
But in that case, if one mixed release of HttpData, cleanAllHttpData and destroy will raised an exception on refCnt during cleanAllHttpData (since object is already released unitary).

So I propose a new solution, still keeping this test (already there in destroy to prevent such an issue but on undecodedChunk only).

@normanmaurer WDYT?
This solution is then homogeneous between Disk, Mixed and Memory based factories of course (same behaviour).
Note that either letting at it was or changing it brings an issue:
- keeping as it was: Disk based and Memory based have not the same behaviiour when releasing items, and the issue is then what to do when in Mixed mode
- changing it: they will behave the same but Memory based will lead to "virtual" lost of memory based items after destroying the decoder, when it was not the case before (the user shall call it when needed)

I'm a bit in the middle:
- making the two acting the same way
- chaning a behaviour which could break some existing codes

My proposal would be: (not the current code)
- not changing behaviour (so not having the Memory based cleaning HttpData by calling forcingly the reslease method (removing lines 953 to 957 in HttpPostMultipartRequestDecoder)
- but keeping refCnt guards such as, at least, no exception are raised while release is beeing called under the wood already (disk based mainly), so minor changes, just as the one you peek (keeping the other changes)

@normanmaurer WDYT?
@normanmaurer I choose, for now until other notice, that my new proposal could be a good tradeof:
- keeping current behaviours, even if not really the same on both sides, in order to keep the current existing client codes functional
- adding the relevant guards when releasing buffers cince they are not equivalent everywhere but will tend to allow the same kind of usage, whatever Memory, Mixed or Disk based, and believing that thos guards prevent errors that are not "client" faults, but underlying implementations that are not aligned to each other (mainly Disk and Memory based)

@normanmaurer I guess that I leave as current code is?

Behaviours are differents between Disk and Memory based Factories, but this small fix allows to at least prevent exception to be thrown when a user release twice a buffer with the "almost" impossibility to know why (in particular in Mixed mode, where the user will have to choose the way to release it according to its real status, Disk or Memory based).

Fixing the different behavious would lead to a major change in the contract for long in place (Disk based releases virtually all underlying buffers/files, while Memory based does not except if calling explicitely `HttpData.release()`). Without this "guard", one can have refCnt exception in Mixed mode while trying to release each HttpData and then destroying the decoder (which brings the destruction of HttpData based on disk, giving the exception).

I believe we should not change the behaviour, but we can prevent internal exception due to two different implementations with this simple guard.

what if serialization on this msg failed as well? do we want to add a try catch here, if this exception failed to serialize, we flatten it to a string, or a minimal Exception to return to the caller, stating serialization issue happened?
SendMessage just enqueues the message, so no serialization happens at that stage. If this also fails to be serialized then something is very wrong.
In the case that we're failing while sending a request message, the rejection will be handled locally and therefore won't be serialized. For response messages, the entire message body is being replaced by a different exception, so if that doesn't work then we have big issues (catastrophe).

If the message still fails then it could be a RequestContext issue, but that means that we won't even be able to deserialize message headers (so we have no information to go by)

Longer term, I'd like to move RequestContext out of the headers for this very reason.

Effectively I just moved the `!offscreenSubtreeWasHidden` check here which caused the whole block to be dedented. 

1. this code is responsible for destroying insertion and layout effects (passive effects are destroyed in `commitPassiveUnmountOnFiber`)
2. layout effects are guaranteed to be already destroyed when `offscreenSubtreeWasHidden` because they are destroyed by `disappearLayoutEffects` immediately~ when the tree gets hidden. We can't destroy them here unconditionally because that would sometimes destroy them twice
3. and yet we need to destroy insertion effects here since they are meant to stay "connected" when the tree gets hidden (that's what @gaearon suggested to me as the fix for this issue). That's why this loop through `updateQueue` is not completely avoidable here right now.

What's the reason that we only infer the value's type for name? Is it because the inference for non-names happens elsewhere and it, therefore, avoids doubl-inference?
> What's the reason that we only infer the value's type for name? Is it because the inference for non-names happens elsewhere and it, therefore, avoids doubl-inference?

We only call `infer_standalone_expression(value)` for *non*-`Name`s. For `Name`s, we call `infer_definition`, which (somewhere down the line) calls `infer_standalone_expression(value)` itself.

So yes, if we would call `infer_standalone_expression(value)` for all targets, we would get double-inference (results e.g. in duplicated diagnostics).

In future, if we add narrowing of attribute types, there could be a distinction between "infer the local type of this attribute expression" and "get the declared type of this attribute", and we would want the latter here, not the former. Which might also obsolete the change in this PR to the inferred type of `Store` attribute expressions. But for now, there is no difference, so it's convenient to just use `infer_attribute_expression` for both.


These will get merged when you run the `isort` rules, so most users will never see this state (or will have it immediately corrected), but I guess it's not ideal (and it does mean that if you're using the LSP, you need to take one Code Action per member).

What do you think if , based on the `file.size` available here, we use one or another. > it does not set the resource_type of the file.

Where do you read that? I only see that their [docs on chunked upload](https://cloudinary.com/documentation/upload_images#chunked_asset_upload) mention:

> By default, when using the upload_large method, files are uploaded as raw files if the resource_type parameter is not specified. For more details about the resource_type option, see Asset types.

Which is quite the opposite - they automatically set the type to "raw". Even so, this is done within the `upload_large` method, [here](https://github.com/cloudinary/cloudinary_npm/blob/966b0f50ba6229df0d35f19138adaca58820414c/lib/uploader.js#L67). The `upload_chunked_stream` does not seem to wiggle with `resource_type` in any way, just as `upload_stream` which Strapi uses now. I.e. there should be no practical difference between `upload_stream` and `upload_chuncked_stream`. I don't observe any problems or changes using `upload_chuncked_stream` on our production.

> If the resource_type parameter is not set when uploading an asset to Cloudinary, the asset is still uploaded successfully. However, Cloudinary may not apply default optimizations and transformations to the uploaded asset for display via the URL. For example, if you provide a URL for an image without specifying the resource_type, you won't be able to apply image-specific transformations like resizing, cropping, or adding effects to the image.

> Furthermore, there is the [upload_large](https://cloudinary.com/documentation/upload_images#chunked_asset_upload) method, which instead of having a default chunk size of 20MB (upload_chunked_stream), it optimizes that value.

`upload_large` cannot take a stream - it only takes a file-system  path to a file or an URL. This sucks for generators outside of systemd's codebase since they won't have access to this function. Can't we set up a decrypted credentials directory for generators as well?
that would mean decrypting *all* passed credentials unconditionally. That's not only problematic because decrypting all this sensitive data of which most might not be consumed at all by the generators means we reveal secrets to way more code than necessary. more problematic is that creds are bound to TPM, and come with PCR policies by default, which can be bound to runtime phases. we should not try to decrypt those, since that's slow, and will quite possibly fail (simply because the cred in question was itended to be unlocked at a different time), and increase "dictionary lockout" counters in the tpm and so on (which will fuck up the object entirely).

generators currently run with almost full system privileges, they are conceptually more part of pid 1 than part of the OS payload. They run in the /init.scope/ cgroup and such. Hence I think it's somewhat OK to have a slightly higher level of requirement on this. But yeah, it's not ideal.

I think for now, people should just shell out to "systemd-creds --system cat" and it will do the right thing... One of those days we should maybe offer some form of IPC API for this in PID 1 or so dunno.

For the record, I hate this. But I think it's a necessary evil, and I tried to limit how exposed this is by making it only available through the impl type.

### Why do we need it?
- The "default" tag is visibly bound to the `IsDefaultScheme` getter above, which queries the model to know the answer.
- When the user clicks the "Set as default" button, we update the "base layer" (yes, I'm still calling it that haha) to have the new color scheme as the default. The key thing here is that the base layer stores the _name_ not the CS object. So we _need_ to query the settings model to answer the question "are we the default?". 
- So now we have a problem: the model updated, but the view model has no idea what's changed. We need the view model to refresh it's value for `IsDefaultScheme`.
- the solution: in `ColorSchemesPageViewModel`, we go through all the schemes and notify that `IsDefaultScheme` has changed.
- alternatively, why not store `IsDefaultScheme` as an observable bool: decided against this because this just makes constructing the list of scheme VMs nasty. This also means that we're not "pure"-ly knowing if we're the default scheme. We're just caching whatever the parent VM tells us, which just feels icky.

You need to set the hostname, otherwise mbedTLS will reject the server certificate. The certificate from echo samples was issued for the `localhost` so the following will do the job:
```
setsockopt(client_fd, SOL_TLS, TLS_HOSTNAME, "localhost", sizeof("localhost"));
```
Hmm... if `CONFIG_NET_HOSTNAME_ENABLE` is not set, should a TLS example always fall back to "localhost" then? That could be one of the issues I was having with Greybus. 
It has nothing to do with `CONFIG_NET_HOSTNAME_ENABLE` - `TLS_HOSTNAME` option specifies the name of the host for which the certificate was issued for (which is part of the certificate itslef, so called "Common Name"). When reaching some public server, it should match the hostname you connect to (see https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/master/samples/net/sockets/http_get/src/http_get.c#L105 for example).

Here, the certificates used by echo were created with Common Name set to `localhost`, so we need to use this name here.

It's also possible to skip hostname validation by explicitly setting this option to a NULL string, but it's isn't considered as safe practice.

I had a look at this. It seems like it is not straight forward to discard an event based upon the data status of the report. 
As an example some drivers pull the event header first before deciding if the event should be discarded or not: https://github.com/zephyrproject-rtos/zephyr/blob/main/drivers/bluetooth/hci/h4.c#L158. That is, the decision of discarding an event is made before we know if the data status is marked with complete or not. 

I see three options:
1. Never discard extended advertising reports
2. Mark complete extended advertising reports as discardable only if we have the data status information available. 
3. Modify every HCI driver to always read the entire event before deciding if it should be discarded. We may consider to optimize this to only pull the part of the event that is required to make a decision.

I prefer option (2) for now. We may consider updating some of the HCI drivers to fetch the entire event before deciding if it should be discarded or not.










The discardable property determines which buffer pool the buffer is taken from. We don't want to clog up the system, possibly creating a deadlock, by stealing all the event buffers for advertising events.

Even though it makes sense to group these together, I do not think using an enum is a good way to do it. 
Enums contains a finite number of possible values - hence you would need to suppress compiler warnings to be able to OR multiple values together - or you would need to typecast the result back to the enum type - which could result in errors if the compiler have made assumptions about the finite number of values (for example a switch statement do not need a default case when doing switch on an enum type and all possible cases are handled). I do not know if newer c-versions have support for this though?
I have seen others define a type and then typecast to this in the define:

`typedef uint32_t bt_audio_loc_t`
`#define BT_AUDIO_LOCATION_FRONT_LEFT ((bt_audio_loc_t) BIT(0))`

then you can use bt_audio_loc_t rather than uint32_t for function parameters that expect audio location(s). And you can freely OR multiple together as the result would still be uint32_t.

To group them together in the API documentation use the `@name Audio Locations @{ ... @} `grouping feature
I hear your concerns and see what issues this may cause. 

The reasoning behind this approach is:

1. (named) `enum`s provide an easy way for an API to express the "group" of values expected, without relying on doxygen-specific grouping
2. https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/v4.10/process/coding-style.html#macros-enums-and-rtl (which we follow) express that `enum`s are preferred for groups of constants
3. This is the approach (although without the name) used by e.g. `BT_LE_ADV_OPT_`, and other option `enum`s in `bluetooth.h`. 

If we see compiler issues, then this is indeed something we need to fix. If the issue is caused by using the `enum` type in the APIs, then we can replace the `enum` type with `uint32_t` (similar to how the `BT_LE_ADV_OPT_` are used), and simply add a reference to the `enum` in the function description. 

If the issue is caused by just using the `enum` keyword, then this problem exist in many places in the code base, and we need a general fix as well as an exception to the Linux kernel code guideline. 

Do you have any references to the compiler issues (warnings) you are experiencing that I can read?

I know that you move code only. But I feel we need to rename this to e.g. `bt_audio_stream_init`. The reason is that there is no registration actually
I disagree. 

This works the same way as 
```c
void bt_le_per_adv_sync_cb_register(struct bt_le_per_adv_sync_cb *cb);
void bt_conn_cb_register(struct bt_conn_cb *cb);
int bt_conn_auth_cb_register(const struct bt_conn_auth_cb *cb);
int bt_conn_auth_info_cb_register(struct bt_conn_auth_info_cb *cb);
void bt_gatt_cb_register(struct bt_gatt_cb *cb);
```
etc. (with the minor difference that many of these are registering the callbacks in a list, instead of a specific object). 



It's not the same, in the examples you mentioned there is actual callback registration, e.g. `bt_conn_cb_register` makes the `struct bt_conn_cb` to be appended to the callback lists, `bt_conn_auth_cb_register` registers `struct bt_conn_auth_cb` where can be just one registered.

In your case, the application provides uninitialized `struct bt_audio_stream *stream` object and is obliged to **initialize** the `ops` member by calling `bt_audio_stream_cb_register`. If the app won't do that, the object is simply unusable
That is a fair point. 

The audio stream callbacks (or ops) are more in line with the ISO or L2CAP channel ops, which are actually never registered with a function. 

This is one the problems I've always had with both the ISO, L2CAP and `bt_audio_stream` APIs: Some fields in the structs are expected to be set directly by the upper layers, and some are expected to be controlled by the stack. This is not only not documented, but even if it was, it would be hard to keep track of. 

Ideally all the fields are the owner's to control (perhaps with exception like `sys_snode_t _node;` which are always stack controlled), or none of them are (and in which case, the struct should be opaque/abstract). 

As long as `bt_audio_stream` is this weird combination of both, it's hard to define a proper API for it. If we want `bt_audio_stream` to follow the APIs of ISO and L2CAP, then we should simply remove `bt_audio_stream_cb_register`

It seems like you explicitly need this to match the type of `size_t`, except signed? What newlib/picolibc do is a clever hack which works on both clang and gcc:
```
#ifdef __SIZE_TYPE__
/* If __SIZE_TYPE__ is defined (gcc) we define ssize_t based on size_t.
   We simply change "unsigned" to "signed" for this single definition
   to make sure ssize_t and size_t only differ by their signedness. */
#define unsigned signed
typedef __SIZE_TYPE__ _ssize_t;
#undef unsigned
#else
...
```
Stick your existing definitions inside the `#else` clause.

Every `off_t` is changed to `k_off_t`, meaning the system footprint will increase. Is this really correct?
Just for illustration, I would normally expect to see `k_off_t` used in thread stacks.

If an application used a new `k_off_t` in every function (which would be unusual), and the stack was 25 function calls deep (which is an exceptionally deep stack), then there would only be 100 bytes of additional stack space used. That average case would be significantly less.

So not too bad.

The benefit is, of course, that Zephyr would always be guaranteed to handle any practically sized file, even if it's 20 GiB or something crazy like that.

The down-side is that architectures would need 2 or more instructions to emulate 64-bit operations (sometimes many more), which would increase flash usage.

E.g. the `samples/subsys/nvs` app increases by 1.1 kB when building for `nrf5340dk/nrf5340/cpuapp`, which I think could be concerning for some.
Moved the Kconfig option to the kernel and renamed `CONFIG_OFFSET_64BIT`, much like `CONFIG_TIMEOUT_64BIT`.

It is not enabled by default, but defers the decision to the user.

At least that sets us up for future-proofing `k_off_t`, @keith-packard.
